findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a multilobulated, multicystic mass with solid components centered in the suprasellar cistern, frequently with an intrasellar component [1-3]. The mass demonstrates heterogeneous signal on MRI, often with high T1 and T2 signal within the cystic portions indicating proteinaceous or cholesterol-rich content, alongside nodular or peripheral calcifications visible on CT [1, 4, 5].
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include prominent enhancement of the solid components and the cyst walls following contrast administration [6]. There is often associated mass effect, such as displacement or compression of the optic chiasm and the third ventricle, the latter potentially leading to obstructive hydrocephalus [1, 7].
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of the mass being oriented strictly along the visual pathways without sellar invasion, which would point toward an optic glioma [8]. Furthermore, there is no synchronous mass in the pineal region or restricted diffusion to suggest a germinoma [9, 10].
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a mixed solid and cystic suprasellar mass with prominent calcifications in a pediatric patient [3, 6, 8].

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Craniopharyngioma, given the classic triad of solid components, cysts, and calcifications in a child [2, 3, 6]. The adamantinomatous variant is particularly common in this age group and characteristically contains cholesterol-rich cysts that return high T1 signal [1, 5].
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Germ cell tumor (GCT), however germinomas are typically homogeneous, solid masses that lack the prominent calcifications and large cystic components seen here [3, 10]. Additionally, germinomas often exhibit restricted diffusion due to dense cellularity and may present with synchronous pineal lesions, which are absent in this case [9, 10].
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Rathke cleft cyst, given that it can present as a cystic sellar or suprasellar mass, and confusing it with a craniopharyngioma could lead to overly aggressive surgical resection rather than simple drainage [5, 11, 12]. However, Rathke cleft cysts are typically entirely cystic without solid enhancing components or calcifications [11, 12].

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the mass's relationship to critical adjacent structures, particularly the optic chiasm, hypothalamus, and third ventricle, to guide surgical planning [1, 6].
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to evaluate the growth rate of the cysts and to identify any evolving secondary hydrocephalus [7].
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain and sella with contrast, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the cystic elements, identifying the exact relationship to the optic pathways, and assessing for any synchronous lesions to fully evaluate the differentials [6, 8, 9].
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences to assess the enhancing solid nodular components and cyst walls, alongside T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences to evaluate mass effect and any peritumoral edema or hydrocephalus [5-7].
management.intervention: If tissue diagnosis needed, I would specify an image-guided or endoscopic transsphenoidal or craniotomy approach, although the striking combination of cysts and calcification on imaging is often sufficiently characteristic to proceed directly to surgical resection [6].
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Pediatric Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the degree of third ventricular compression, optic chiasm displacement, and the extent of the solid and cystic components to facilitate optimal surgical or radiotherapeutic management [1, 6, 7].

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a multilobulated, multicystic mass with solid components centered in the suprasellar cistern, frequently with an intrasellar component [1-3]. The mass demonstrates heterogeneous signal on MRI, often with high T1 and T2 signal within the cystic portions indicating proteinaceous or cholesterol-rich content, alongside nodular or peripheral calcifications visible on CT [1, 4, 5]. Supporting features include prominent enhancement of the solid components and the cyst walls following contrast administration [6]. There is often associated mass effect, such as displacement or compression of the optic chiasm and the third ventricle, the latter potentially leading to obstructive hydrocephalus [1, 7]. Importantly, there is no evidence of the mass being oriented strictly along the visual pathways without sellar invasion, which would point toward an optic glioma [8]. Furthermore, there is no synchronous mass in the pineal region or restricted diffusion to suggest a germinoma [9, 10]. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a mixed solid and cystic suprasellar mass with prominent calcifications in a pediatric patient [3, 6, 8].

The appearances are most in keeping with Craniopharyngioma, given the classic triad of solid components, cysts, and calcifications in a child [2, 3, 6]. The adamantinomatous variant is particularly common in this age group and characteristically contains cholesterol-rich cysts that return high T1 signal [1, 5]. The principle differential would be Germ cell tumor (GCT), however germinomas are typically homogeneous, solid masses that lack the prominent calcifications and large cystic components seen here [3, 10]. Additionally, germinomas often exhibit restricted diffusion due to dense cellularity and may present with synchronous pineal lesions, which are absent in this case [9, 10]. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Rathke cleft cyst, given that it can present as a cystic sellar or suprasellar mass, and confusing it with a craniopharyngioma could lead to overly aggressive surgical resection rather than simple drainage [5, 11, 12]. However, Rathke cleft cysts are typically entirely cystic without solid enhancing components or calcifications [11, 12].

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the mass's relationship to critical adjacent structures, particularly the optic chiasm, hypothalamus, and third ventricle, to guide surgical planning [1, 6]. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to evaluate the growth rate of the cysts and to identify any evolving secondary hydrocephalus [7]. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the brain and sella with contrast, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the cystic elements, identifying the exact relationship to the optic pathways, and assessing for any synchronous lesions to fully evaluate the differentials [6, 8, 9]. I would specify high-resolution T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences to assess the enhancing solid nodular components and cyst walls, alongside T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences to evaluate mass effect and any peritumoral edema or hydrocephalus [5-7]. If tissue diagnosis needed, I would specify an image-guided or endoscopic transsphenoidal or craniotomy approach, although the striking combination of cysts and calcification on imaging is often sufficiently characteristic to proceed directly to surgical resection [6]. This should go to the Pediatric Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the degree of third ventricular compression, optic chiasm displacement, and the extent of the solid and cystic components to facilitate optimal surgical or radiotherapeutic management [1, 6, 7].
